04 Fulfillment Engines Explained - YouTube.mp4
Do you know the story of McDonald's? Yep, I'm referring to the inexpensive and ubiquitous hamburger restaurant that you can find in nearly every city in every country on planet Earth. Now, as a But foodie, you might not love their burgers. But as an entrepreneur, you have to respect their ingenuity. According to legend, the McDonald's brothers revolutionized the— industry by applying the principles of production line manufacturing to the fast food industry in what became known as the speedy system. But the speedy system wasn't developed in a boardroom or at some innovation conference surrounded by fancy six-figure consultants. Nope, the McDonald's brothers— were struggling entrepreneurs just like the rest of us. So when they didn't have fancy consultants and boardrooms, they grabbed a box of chalk and assembled their entire...
team on an open tennis court. They started by drawing out their proposed restaurant floor plan in chalk and they had all their employees just go. the motions, as though they were making and serving hamburgers to invisible customers. They kept tweaking this process until, after a few iterations, they had it. A visual representation drawn in chalk on a tennis court of a restaurant concept that would be duplicated, not just dozens, not just hundreds. Hundreds, but tens of thousands of times, all over the world. What the McDonald's brothers drew on that San Bernardino tennis court is an example of what we call a fulfillment engine. A fulfillment engine visualizes the process by which customers receive the promised value after the sale is made. But where the McDonald's brothers used chalk and a tennis court, you'll be using sticky notes and a whiteboard.
